mnemonics: STRUCTURE has Many Rooms (members), UNION is One Room for Everyone (shared memory)
keyPoints: A structure (struct) is a user-defined data type that groups different data types under one name, with each member having its own memory.
A union is a user-defined data type that allows storing different data types in the same memory location, but only one member can contain a value at any given time.
Unions are memory efficient but should be used carefully as accessing the wrong member leads to undefined behavior.
commonMistakes: Accessing a union member that was not the most recently written to, resulting in incorrect data interpretation.
Assuming struct and union members are allocated memory in the same way (structs sum their members' sizes, unions use the size of the largest member).
Forgetting to use the '.' operator for structs/unions and the '->' operator for pointers to them, leading to compilation errors.
